coredata.dc:description: Ternary lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) with higher energy density are more vulnerable to thermal runaway (TR) owing to the interior material structure, particularly under abusive conditions. Among various abusive conditions, overheating abuse is widely utilized to trigger TR. In this paper, the thermal-electrochemical characteristics correlations of ternary lithium power cells (Li(Ni0.5Co0.2Mn0.3)O2/C, NCM523; Li(Ni0.6Co0.2Mn0.2)O2/C, NCM622; and Li(Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1)O2/C, NCM811) under adiabatic and TR conditions were systematically studied. Primarily, in order to clarify the impact of nickel content and discharge rate on temperature, the heat generation behaviors of the aforementioned three cells were explored under adiabatic conditions at ambient/higher temperature experimental conditions. Secondly, the thermal-electrochemical properties of three ternary cells during the TR process triggered by heating abuse were assessed. Moreover, the variations trends of voltage, current, and characteristic temperature with time were carefully examined, and the mechanism of TR occurrence and chain reactions are explained. Eventually, the microscopic irreversible damage to the active materials caused by the increased heat production and electrochemical performance deterioration were analyzed. The experimental results indicated that the maximum temperature and temperature rising rate of NCM811 under adiabatic conditions came to the highest value. The autogenous heat onset temperature T1 of NCM811 reached 78.4 °C, which was 2.9 °C and 2.4 °C lower than that of NCM523 and NCM622, respectively. The peak temperature T3 of NCM811 during the TR process achieved 674.44 °C, which was 99.5 °C and 3.81 °C higher than that of NCM523 and NCM622, respectively. Hence，the thermal safety of NCM811 was the worst. Notably, the corresponding micro results showed NCM811 suffered the most severe irreversible damage as a result of TR. This damage was mostly manifested in the collector and negative electrode materials being severely peeled off, as well as the obvious aggregation of negative electrode active materials. The relevant research will provide systematic theoretical guidance and experimental data support for the improvement of thermal safety performance of ternary lithium batteries and modules with high energy density.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ternary lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) with higher energy density are more vulnerable to thermal runaway (TR) owing to the interior material structure, particularly under abusive conditions. Among various abusive conditions, overheating abuse is widely utilized to trigger TR. In this paper, the thermal-electrochemical characteristics correlations of ternary lithium power cells (Li(Ni0.5Co0.2Mn0.3)O2/C, NCM523; Li(Ni0.6Co0.2Mn0.2)O2/C, NCM622; and Li(Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1)O2/C, NCM811) under adiabatic and TR conditions were systematically studied. Primarily, in order to clarify the impact of nickel content and discharge rate on temperature, the heat generation behaviors of the aforementioned three cells were explored under adiabatic conditions at ambient/higher temperature experimental conditions. Secondly, the thermal-electrochemical properties of three ternary cells during the TR process triggered by heating abuse were assessed. Moreover, the variations trends of voltage, current, and characteristic temperature with time were carefully examined, and the mechanism of TR occurrence and chain reactions are explained. Eventually, the microscopic irreversible damage to the active materials caused by the increased heat production and electrochemical performance deterioration were analyzed. The experimental results indicated that the maximum temperature and temperature rising rate of NCM811 under adiabatic conditions came to the highest value. The autogenous heat onset temperature T1 of NCM811 reached 78.4 °C, which was 2.9 °C and 2.4 °C lower than that of NCM523 and NCM622, respectively. The peak temperature T3 of NCM811 during the TR process achieved 674.44 °C, which was 99.5 °C and 3.81 °C higher than that of NCM523 and NCM622, respectively. Hence，the thermal safety of NCM811 was the worst. Notably, the corresponding micro results showed NCM811 suffered the most severe irreversible damage as a result of TR. This damage was mostly manifested in the collector and negative electrode materials being severely peeled off, as well as the obvious aggregation of negative electrode active materials. The relevant research will provide systematic theoretical guidance and experimental data support for the improvement of thermal safety performance of ternary lithium batteries and modules with high energy density.

ce:section.ce:para: Electric vehicles (EVs), with their superior energy-saving and emission-reducing advantages, have been explosively developed around the world [1–8]. As the core component of EVs, the properties of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) will directly affect the performance of EVs, especially the operational safety. Ternary LIBs owing higher energy density contribute to the improvement of driving miles and power density [9–15]. Nevertheless, due to their specific chemical systems and internal structure of the active materials, ternary LIBs will be more prone to trigger thermal runaway (TR) under various abusive conditions (e.g. thermal, electrical and mechanical abuse), furtherly causing serious irreversible damage to the battery [16–20]. Hence, the study referring to TR characteristics of ternary LIBs will be helpful for the improvement of the thermal safety to a great extent.

At present, the TR characteristics of ternary LIBs have been researched by some scholars. Zhu et al. [21] found that TR is unlikely to occur in small heated regions, furtherly obtained that heat generation during the reaction between the anode and the electrolyte of TR cells is the main cause of TR cells. This result could be employed to guide the effective prevention and propagation of LIBs TR. Peng et al. [22] also studied the thermal behaviors of LiCoO2 cells using simulation analysis approach. The conclusion indicated that convective heat transfer led to a strong heat-producing chemical reaction that caused a rapid increase in cell temperature when the oven temperature reached 190◦C under normal air conditions. Xu et al. [23] developed a TR model for LIBs under thermoelectric coupling abuse. The effects of different operating conditions on the internal parameters and TR characteristics were analyzed by comparing the heat source location, charging rate and discharging rate of the LIBs. The results showed that the higher the charging rate, the higher the peak temperature of TR. Ren et al. [24] investigated the relationship between internal short circuit and TR of LIBs under thermal abuse conditions. The thermal contraction of the diaphragm was found to be the cause of the internal short circuit. Additionally, the exothermic reaction between the anode and the electrolyte was identified as the trigger cause for the TR of Li(Ni1/3Co1/3Mn1/3)O2. Feng et al. [25] found that cells with high temperature cycles had better thermal stability than those with low temperature cycles, attributing this improvement to internal structural factors. Kong et al. [26] studied the effect of operating temperature on the TR and combustion behaviors of LIBs, and conducted a series of experiments on thermal abuse of LIBs at different temperatures and cycling rates. The experimental results made clear that the TR onset temperature of the LIBs decreased significantly as the operating temperature decreases from 25 °C to − 10°C. Low temperature cycling resulted in safety valve cracking and early TR onset. Lai et al. [27] conducted and compared the TR propagation (TRP) behavior of LIBs modules under three typical trigger modes (heating, mechanical, and overcharge abuses). The differences in TRP time and triggering temperature for different trigger modes were obvious in the early TRP period, but these differences were gradually eliminated in the late TRP period. Specifically, mechanical abuse realized the shortest trigger time, while the heating trigger had the best overall performance, good LIBs test operability, and lower cost. Consequently, TRP could be suppressed or delayed by improving the structure of the battery pack to reduce the eruption temperature or expel the ejected material to prevent the explosion. Feng et al. [28–30] attempted to determine the mechanism responsible for the formation of {T1, the onset temperature of abnormal heating; T2, the trigger temperature of the TR; T3, the maximum temperature reached by the cell during TR}, finally providing insight into the TR mechanism of LIBs. T1 was caused by a reaction at the anode. The redox reaction generated intense heat after the temperature reached T2, which heated the monomer from T2 to T3. By increasing T1 and decreasing T3, the safety of the cell could also be improved. Feng et al. [31,32] simulated and analyzed the effects of TR trigger temperature, TR heat release from the cell, and other factors on the TR propagation rate by building an aggregate parametric model and a three-dimensional model of TR propagation. The increase of TR trigger temperature means that TR was more difficult to trigger, and the decrease of total TR heat release indicated that the heat transfer to the surrounding battery was reduced, resulting in slowing down the TR propagation. The TR trigger temperature was related to the thermal stability of the battery material, and the total TR heat release decreased as the battery state of charge (SOC) declined. Wang et al. [33] found that the temperature rise rate could significantly characterize the occurrence of TR, and the probability of TR in LIBs was high when the temperature rise rate exceeded 1 °C •s−1. Furthermore, when the average temperature exceeded 80 °C, there was a potential risk of TR in LIBs. The results of the study showed that it needed longer time to trigger TR at high temperatures on account of a slower warm-up rate of LIBs. Mao et al. [34] performed pin penetration experiments on 18650 cells and showed that different locations and depths of pin penetration had specific effects on the TR of the cells. Ren et al. [24] analyzed the effects of different magnification sizes on the cells and found that the effects of different magnification currents on the TR characteristics of the cells were was very small. Feng et al. [35] described the TR characteristics of cells using EV- (accelerating rate calorimeter, ARC). They investigated the propagation of penetration-induced TR in the cell modules. In the ARC test [36], mechanically abused TR propagation had a lower onset temperature and shorter TR trigger time than thermally abused TR. Mechanical and electrical abuses eventually led to overheating. Moreover, Lyu et al. [37] analyzed that conditions such as loose battery connectors and external heating might also lead to local overheating and thus thermal abuse, with extreme high temperatures causing shrinkage and collapse of the diaphragm, resulting in a large-scale internal short circuit. Feng et al. [38] found that under different abuse conditions, overheating abuse was a frequent approach utilized mentioning trigger TR. Wei et al. [39] investigated the influence of different abuse methods (nail penetration, side heating and overcharging) on TR and TRP behaviors of LIBs. They acquired that TRP induced by overcharge abuse was found to be the most dangerous, followed by overheating abuse, and finally, mechanical penetration abuse. Sharp et al. [40] determined the correlation between the cell geometry and its thermal behavior correlation. The outcome indicated that the rate of TR propagation within the cell was usually the highest with increasing diameter compared to thermal or internal short-circuit abuse. Hu et al. [41] studied TR coupling the thermal abuse and charging. The data showed that with the increase of the current rate, the lower the TR onset temperature, the higher the thermal risk and the maximum temperature. Jiang et al. [42] found that the thermal runaway propagation process is divided into initial stage, rapid expansion stage and explosion stage. Hu et al. [43] established a model for thermal abuse of LIBs modules. The temperature propagation characteristics of the battery pack under high temperature heat source were discussed and analyzed. The results showed that the TR of the pack was consistent at different surface heat source temperatures. When TR occurred at 453 K, the temperature distribution was only affected the location of the surface heat source. Duh et al. [44] investigated the TR of Panasonic 21700 Li(Ni0.8Co0.15Al0.05)O2 LIBs under thermal abuse condition. The TR consequences of Panasonic 21700 Li(Ni0.8Co0.15Al0.05)O2 with 25% capacity could not be neglected because of its maximum temperature of about 600 °C and maximum self-heating value of 10,000 °C•min−1. Zhang et al. [45] found that overcharging battery may cause an increase in the internal resistance and EIS spectrum，leading to higher heat production rates, which was more likely to trigger TR. In a word, all the aforementioned papers mainly focused on the TR mechanisms, heat generation behavior and electrochemical properties of the battery during TR process. Nevertheless, the microstructures variations and the root causes of the electrode materials from the micro perspective after TR are rarely referred. As known, the electrochemical properties and heat generation during the charge/discharge processes of the LIBs were mutually influenced. The rapid increase of heat generation will cause a serious degradation of the electrochemical performance of the battery, finally the internal materials itself. Hence, the studying on the underlying causes of the degradation of electrochemical performance and the increase of heat production during TR period are very essential.

In this paper, the correlation of thermal-electrochemical properties of ternary LIBs with different nickel contents under adiabatic and TR conditions were systematically investigated. Furthermore, the essential causes resulting from the performance degradation were concretely described. In the first place, the maximum temperature of three ternary cells with different nickel contents at different discharge rates under adiabatic conditions at room/ higher temperature were experimentally tested, and the changes of temperature during the discharging process were analyzed, as well as the influence mechanism of nickel content and discharge rate on temperature. Secondly, the TR characteristics of three types of cells triggered under thermal abuse conditions were evaluated, and the changes of voltage, current and characteristic temperature in the process of TR were tested. Furtherly, and the mechanism of TR triggered during the electrochemical reaction was elaborated. Eventually, the macroscopic tests and microscopic characterization of three different nickel-containing cores after TR occurrence were carried out. The macroscopic appearance changes of the cores before and after the TR were characterized. The related research will help to improve the thermal safety of such chemical system LIBs in actual applications.

In order to explore the correlation of different nickel contents on the thermal-electrochemical properties of the cell, three different nickel contents of 18650 type ternary cells were selected for this experiment, namely (Li(Ni0.5Co0.2Mn0.3)O2/C, NCM523, Dongguan Sinowatt Co., Ltd, Dongguan, China; Li(Ni0.6Co0.2Mn0.2)O2/C, NCM622, Shenzhen Bak Power Battery Co., Ltd, China; and Li(Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1)O2/C, NCM811, Zhengzhou Bak Power Battery Co., Ltd, Zhengzhou, China). It should be noted that the same type of ternary battery used for different experiments in this paper has good consistency. The technical parameters of the three types of batteries are shown in Table 1, and the three types of batteries are shown in Fig. 1.

The schematic diagram of the charge/discharge experimental system is shown in Fig. 2. A thermostat (BTH-80 C, Bell Measurement Equipment Co., Ltd., Dongguan, China; temperature range: −70°C to 150 °C) was employed to maintain constant operating conditions of 25 °C and 55 °C. The battery test cabinet (CT-3001 W-50 V/120 A-NTF, Shenzhen Xinwei Co., Ltd., China) was used for experimental cell charging and discharging tests, allowing real-time accurate monitoring of cell voltage and current with a 1 s acquisition interval. T-type thermocouple (OMEGA-type TT-T-30-SLE-1 M; accuracy = ± 0.1 °C) was glued to the geometric position of the cell in order to measure the temperature (as shown in Fig. 3) with a test interval of 1 s. The raw temperature data were recorded with a temperature patrol instrument (34,901 A, Key Aim Technology Co., Ltd., China). ARC (temperature range −50°C ∼300 °C, heating rate 0.02 °C /min∼200 °C /min, exothermic detection sensitivity min. 0.02 °C). was mainly adopted for TR testing.

For examining the influence of nickel content on the electrochemical properties of three types of cells, NCM523, NCM622 and NCM811 were measured at room temperature (25 °C) to analyze the change pattern of charging/discharging voltage plateau and discharge capacity under uninterrupted charging and discharging cycles. The discharged capacity is calculated according to Eq. (1). The specific charging and discharging cycle processes were shown as follows:
Rest stage: 30 min.
Galvanostatic charging stage: batteries were charged at a 1.0 C charge rate until the voltage reached the cut-off limit of 4.2 V.
Potentiostatic charging stage: batteries were continued to be charged until the current declined to 234 mA.
Rest stage: 10 min.
Constant-current discharging stage: were used in a constant-current discharge (4.5 C) mode until the cut-off voltage came to 2.75 V.
Rest stage: 20 min.

Cycle times: 20.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature variations of cells under adiabatic condition
ce:section.ce:para: The NCM523, NCM622, and NCM811 cells were charged at a constant current of 1.0 C to a cut-off voltage of 4.2 V, followed by a constant voltage charge to a cut-off current of 234 mA at room/higher (25 °C/55 °C) temperature conditions. After that, the cells were discharged at 0.5 C, 1.0 C and 2.0 Crates to analyze the maximum temperature change pattern of the cells with the boost of discharge process. The ARC device was heated to 25 °C/55 °C in "H" mode for the experiment, ensuring even battery heating. The battery discharged at rates of 0.5 C, 1.0 C, and 2.0 C, followed by entering the "W" mode. After "W" mode, the system switched to "S" mode to check heating rates. If the rate exceeded 0.03 °C•min−1, indicating self-generated heat, the adiabatic mode recorded changes. If the rate was less than 0.03 °C•min−1, the system cycled through "W-S" until the battery self-heated. The detailed ARC operation procedures are shown in Fig. 4. The experimental test of the cell inside ARC is exhibited in Fig. 5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TR characteristics test of different nickel-content cells
ce:section.ce:para: Under adiabatic conditions, the T1, T2, T3 and the temperature rising rate of NCM523, NCM622 and NCM811(SOC=100%) were examined at a temperature rise rate of 2 °C•min−1 during the thermal abuse process. Ulteriorly, the variations of voltage and current with discharge time were also elaborated. It should be noted that the experimental conditions of the aforementioned cells remained consistent. The idiographic experimental procedures were depicted in Section 2.4.1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microscopic morphological characterization of the cells after TR
ce:section.ce:para: After the TR experiments, the normal battery (NB) and thermal runaway battery (TRB) (SOC=0%) were placed in a glove box and dissected under an argon atmosphere at 25 °C. The procedures were shown in Fig. 6. The grey insulating plastic film on the outside of the cell was firstly stripped, and then the bare metal steel shell was slowly peeled off with tweezers. During the process, the caps of the positive and negative electrodes firstly fell off. After that, the unique winding structure of the diaphragm, anode and cathode electrode slices was clearly observed. The active materials attached to anode and cathode electrode collectors should be carefully separated in order to prevent short circuits. The obtained intact collectors were carefully cleaned with cotton swabs at 25 °C. The conductive powders were then carefully extracted from the aforementioned collectors with a utility knife and dried in an oven (DHG-9070 A, Shanghai Kimung Co., Ltd.) at 80 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, the active materials were furtherly morphological characterized using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Hitachi S-3400 N-II, Japan). The above operations were carried out in an argon atmosphere. The dissection processes of NCM 523 are particularly shown in Fig. 6, which were consistent with that of NCM622 and NCM811.

After removing the cap, it could be observed that the active materials of TRB occurred severe leakage phenomenon compared to that of NB. The negative collector copper foil of TRB became wrinkled and the color turned into black due to high temperature. What was worse, the positive electrode active materials were damaged more seriously, which was impossible to extract them from the collector. Consequently, the follow-up microscopic characterization of electrode materials mainly focused on the negative collector and active materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties of different nickel-content cells
Voltage plateau changes of different nickel content cells
ce:section.ce:para: As known, the whole charging stage include two stages containing of constant-current and constant-voltage charging periods. In this chapter, charging-voltage curves were tested and compared to analyze the impact of different nickel content on charging voltage during the interrupted charging-discharging cycles. It could be concluded from Fig. 7 that the voltage of NCM523, NCM622 and NCM811 kept a creeping up trend with the increase of charging time. Nevertheless, there was an adverse trend during the discharging period. Before the discharge curve reached 2300 s, it decreased gently and the discharge speed increased significantly after 2300 sIt was noted that the constant-current discharge time of NCM811 was the shortest compared to that of NCM622 and NCM523. Whether charging or discharging, the charging and discharging rates of LIBs with different nickel contents were found to be accelerated with the increase of nickel content in the later stages, and the time required to reach the same voltage stage was reduced with the increase of nickel content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discharge capacity variations of different nickel content cells
ce:section.ce:para: From Fig. 8, it could be concluded that as the increase of cycles number, the discharge capacity of NCM811 decreased by 6.2% from 2849.4 mAh at the 1st cycle to 2672.1 mAh at the 20th cycle, exhibiting an overall decreasing trend. While NCM622 might be caused by incomplete discharge at the early stage, and NCM622 and NCM523 both showed fluctuations around the average value at the later stage. What was noteworthy was that compared with NCM622 and NCM523, the average discharge capacity of NCM811 was 4.9% and 9.1% higher, respectively. The corresponding conclusion was consistent with the references [46,47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat generation characteristics of different nickel content cells
Temperature rise test of different nickel content cells under adiabatic conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 showed that the peak temperature curves of LIBs under adiabatic conditions under room temperature of 25 °C. As could be seen from Fig. 9, the maximum temperatures of NCM523, NCM622 and NCM811 came to 36.5 °C, 36.5 °C and 36.1 °C at a lower discharge rate of 0.5 C. At 1.0 C, the highest temperatures of NCM523, NCM622 and NCM811 were 42.9 °C, 45.4 °C and 47.3 °C. For NCM811, it was obvious that the peak temperature achieved the maximum value, which indicated the heat generation behaviors were the most severe, easily leading to TR. At a 2.0 C high discharge rate, the maximum temperature and temperature rise rate of NCM523 reached 56.7 °C and 1.026 °C•min−1, respectively. Moreover, the maximum temperature and temperature rising speed of NCM622 were 68.8 °C and 1.436 °C•min−1, respectively. The maximum temperature of NCM811 was 70.9 ℃, which was 25% and 3% higher than that of NCM523 and NCM622. The temperature rising rate of NCM811 was 1.556 °C•min−1,which was 51.6% and 8.4% higher than that of NCM523 and NCM622, respectively.

In order to describe the heat generation behaviors of aforementioned LIBs under higher temperature condition, the LIBs were conducted for experimental measurement at a temperature of 55 °C. Fig. 10 displayed the peak temperature fluctuations of NCM523, NCM622 and NCM811 under 55 °C temperature condition. As could be obtained from Fig. 10, the maximum temperatures of NCM523, NCM622 and NCM811 were 61.2 °C, 61.9 °C and 61.1 °C at a 0.5 C lower discharge rate. When the discharge rate achieved to 1.0 C, the maximum temperatures of NCM523, NCM622 and NCM811 were 68.4 °C, 71.7 °C and 73.3 °C, and the temperature rise rates were 0.213 °C•min−1, 0.286 °C•min−1 and 0.31 °C•min−1. At a 2.0 C higher discharge rate, the maximum temperature and temperature rise rate of NCM811 were 96.6 °C and 1.4597 °C•min−1, respectively, compared with that of NCM523 and NCM622, the uppermost temperature increased by 58% and 40.4%, and the temperature rise rate increased by 42.3% and 1.7%, respectively. At 0.5 C and 1.0 C discharge the ohmic heat and polarization heat and irreversible heat proportion is equivalent, so the temperature difference between the three batteries at 0.5 C and 1.0 C is not obvious [48]. Different from the ohmic heat of high-rate discharge above 1.0 C, which accounts for most of the battery heat generation [49]. The NCM811 battery has more Li+ stored inside compared to the NCM523 and NCM622. If NCM811 moves more Li+ than NCM523 and NCM622 during the same time period, its internal resistance will be reduced and heat production will be correspondingly lower. The macroscopic results show that the maximum temperature of NCM811 is slightly lower than that of NCM622 and NCM523, especially at lower discharge rates. With the constantly augment of discharge rate, NCM811 exhibited the worst thermogenic effect, which was consistent with that of Fig. 9 and furtherly increased the risk of TR. Hence, the TR characteristics of the above-mentioned LIBs will be elaborated in the following sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature rise rate variations of different nickel content cells under TR conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 11 indicated the electrochemical properties and temperature changes curves of NCM523 during the whole TR process. As could be seen in Fig. 11, when the temperature rise rate exceeded 0.03 °C/min, the self-generated heat temperature T1 of NCM523 reached 81.3 °C. The TR trigger temperature T2 of NCM523 was 184.3 °C. In the initial stage of the test, the cavity temperature rose at a uniform rate of 2 °C /min. Moreover, the heat was transferred to the cell through air convection, resulting in the temperature increment. Before the cell reached the TR trigger temperature T2, the cell voltage dropped abruptly to oscillate around 2 V. This was due to the local heat contraction of the cell diaphragm, causing the Li+ transmission channel closure and a slight internal short circuit. When the battery temperature was 119.5 °C, a sudden decrease in the temperature rise rate was clearly observed, this was because the battery pressure relief valve was broken, and the high temperature gas inside the battery took away part of the heat, resulting in a sudden drop in battery temperature, and the high temperature gas was from the SEI film decomposition reaction, micro short circuit and electrolyte gasification. After the rupture of the pressure relief valve, the active material inside the battery came into direct contact with the air, which intensified the reaction of the internal material and led to the battery temperature continue rising. When the battery temperature rise rate continued to be higher than 1 °C•s−1, the battery internal side reactions were intense, the battery temperature rose rapidly, and TR might occur at any time. When the TR occurred, the temperature rise rate came up to 4264.71 °C•min−1, the highest temperature T3 also instantly reached 574.94 °C.

Fig. 12 showed the electrochemical-heat generation properties variations of NCM622 during the whole TR process. As described in Fig. 12, the T1 of NCM622 battery reached 80.8 °C, with a temperature rise rate of more than 0.03 °C•min−1 as the basis for judgment, and then the chamber temperature rose at a uniform rate of 2 °C /min during the initial stage of the test. Before the cell TR trigger temperature T2 came to 171.7 °C, the cell voltage suddenly drops to oscillate around 2 V. This was due to the local heat contraction of the battery diaphragm, which closed the Li+ transport channel and caused a slight internal short circuit. When the battery temperature was 119.5 °C, a sudden decrease in temperature rise rate occurred. The reason causing the aforementioned phenomenon was similar to that of NCM523. After the rupture of the pressure relief valve, the active material inside the battery had direct contact with the air, which intensified the reaction of the internal material, resulting in the battery temperature continue rising. After the occurrence of TR temperature rise rate came up to 9069.1 °C•min−1, T3 also instantly reached 670.63 °C, which rose by 645.63 °C, reaching 25.83 times.

Fig. 13 displayed the voltage and temperature variations of NCM811 during the whole TR process. As elaborated in Fig. 13, the NCM811 battery reached the initial TR trigger point at 78.4 °C, when the temperature increasing rate exceeded 0.03 °C /min as a judgment basis. And the cell was judged to have reached the TR trigger stage under the condition that the temperature rise rate continued to exceed 1 °C /min, and then in the initial stage of the test, the cavity temperature increased at a uniform rate of 2 °C /min, and the heat was transferred to the cell through air convection to warm it up. Before the cell came to the initial TR trigger temperature T2 139.9 °C, the cell voltage dropped abruptly to oscillate around 2 V. This was due to the characteristic heat contraction of the battery diaphragm, which closes the Li+ signal channel and causes an internal short circuit while the battery temperature was maintained. At 120.7 °C, it could be seen that the battery temperature suddenly drops due to the break of the battery cover, and after the pressure suction cup breaks, the material inside the battery was directly exposed to the air, resulting in an increase in the battery temperature. During the occurrence of TR period, the temperature rise rate of NCM811 cell came up to 9420.33 °C•min−1 and T3 also instantly reached 676.44 °C. The temperature rose by 651.44 °C, reaching 26.06 times.

The T2 of NCM811 achieved 139.9 °C, which was 47 °C and 31.8 °C lower than that of NCM523 and NCM622, respectively. However, and the temperature rise rate of NCM811 was much higher than that of NCM523 and NCM622 within the temperature range of 150–200 °C. Compared with NCM523 and NCM622, it could be seen that the T1 and T2 of NCM811 were much lower and its temperature rise rate was also the most dramatic accompanied by the highest T3. The corresponding data made clear that high nickel content batteries were less thermally stable and were prone to cause TR at relatively lower temperatures and higher heat release during TR, eventually leading to an increased risk of TR[50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Appearance investigation of power cells after TR
ce:section.ce:para: The changes of thermo-chemical characteristics in the TR process will inevitably cause damage to the appearance of the battery and the morphology of the active substances inside. In the following manuscript, the external morphology of the cells and the microscopic morphology of the internal conductive materials after TR were furtherly studied. Fig. 14 showed the appearance of NCM523, NCM622 and NCM811 before and after TR. From Fig. 14, the three cells possessing different nickel contents had different degrees of damage from various perspectives, and the top cover of NCM811 had been deformed and illegible. From the Fig. 14(a), Fig. 14(d) and Fig. 14(g), the exterior damage of NCM811 was more severe compared to that of NCM523 and NCM622, probably caused by the highest maximum temperature and temperature rise rate of NCM811 during TR. From Fig. 14(b), Fig. 14(e) and Fig. 14(h), it could be seen that the damage at the electrode of the NCM811 cell caused by TR was the most dramatic. It could be observed from Fig. 14 (c), Fig. 14(f) and Fig. 14(i) that the active material emitted from the inside of the cell also increased with the increase of the nickel content. Hence, the higher the nickel content, the higher the maximum temperature and temperature rise rate of the cell, the more serious the damage to the outer surface of the NCM811 cell compared to the NCM523 and NCM622, which was consistent with the experimental data in Section 3.2.

After TR, the positive collectors of the three different nickel content cells have been broken and mixed together indistinguishably, so only the performance of the negative collectors before and after TR were investigated and compared in the following manuscript. Fig. 15 compared of the appearance of negative collector before and after TR of three different nickel content cells. From Fig. 15(a), Fig. 15(d) and Fig. 15(g), it could be observed clearly that NCM811 no longer possessed the prototype anode material compared with that of NCM523 and NCM622, and the upper part was even partly missing. From Fig. 15(b), Fig. 15(e)and Fig. 15(h), it could be obtained that NCM523 still owed a coiled structure, while NCM622 and NCM811 no longer had a complete coiled structure and the negative electrode material had become fragile. From Fig. 15(c), Fig. 15(f) and Fig. 15(i), NCM523 and NCM622 are also damaged to some extent, and the damage of NCM622 battery winding configuration is higher than that of NCM523 battery. Notely, the anode collector of NCM811 suffered the worst damage and exhibited the minimum integrity. The reason leading to damage to negative electrode materials was due to the decomposition reaction of SEI film, micro-short circuit and high temperature gas released from the gasification of electrolyte, which was closely related to the amount of nickel content. This is similar to the phenomenon found by Zhang et al. [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure of the negative electrodes after TR
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 16 described the internal morphology of the negative electrodes of three different nickel contents cells before and after TR. From Fig. 16, it could be seen from Fig. 16(a), Fig. 16(b) and Fig. 16(c) that the active substances on the negative electrode sheets of NCM523, NCM622 and NCM811 before TR were tightly ordered and evenly distributed. After TR, Fig. 16(d) showed that the active substance of NCM523 was slightly stripped from the pole sheet, and the conductive substance was slightly disordered. With the increase of nickel content, the stripping of the active substance and the pole sheet became more obvious and severe, the structure of the active substance displayed mixed and disorderly arrangement, and the overall structure also collapsed (Fig. 16(e)). For the NCM811 cell, the above phenomenon was the most serious. The separation distance between the active material and the polar plate is increasing, and the distribution of the active material was seriously and completely disordered (Fig. 16(f)). The irreversible damage of the internal morphology of the active substance will directly affect the electrochemical energy storage capacity of the battery and furtherly hinder the electrochemical reaction processes. The severity of the damage directly determined the decline of the energy storage capacity.

Fig. 17 displayed the microstructure of the negative conductive active substance of NCM523, NCM622 and NCM811 before and after TR. The tight uniform and orderly arrangement of uniform particles were observed in Fig. 17(a), Fig. 17(b) and Fig. 17(c). In addition, compared with the smooth surface of the conductive material of NCM523, NCM622 and NCM811 before TR (Fig. 17(a), Fig. 17(b) and Fig. 17(c)), the surface of the conductive material in the negative electrode plate of the cell after TR became rough and there were some loose gaps (Fig. 17(d), Fig. 17(e) and Fig. 17(f)). With the increase of nickel content, the agglomeration phenomenon of negative active material was getting more obvious, resulting in an increasing gap. Nevertheless, the microstructure of NCM811 anode material suffered the most serious irreversible damage after TR, resulting the most severe electrochemical energy storage capacity reduction. This phenomenon is similar to Ren et al. [52] who found that both the appearance and internal structure of the battery were damaged when TR occurred in the pouch battery.

The examination of the microstructure of various nickel-containing cells, both pre and post TR, reveals a consistent occurrence of electrode particle breakage and stripping during LIB TR. This phenomenon leads to a reduction in lithium ion embedding and electrolyte diffusion. Macroscopically, the morphology of the NCM 811 negative electrode is distorted, with the most severe separation observed between the negative electrode material and the copper collector at the microscopic level. A review of pertinent literature highlights that both electrical and mechanical abuse can adversely affect the internal battery structure, thereby influencing electrochemical performance and elevating the risk of heat generation [53,54].

In this paper, the electrochemical performance parameters and temperature variations of three kinds of nickel-containing batteries during normal charge/discharge processes under adiabatic condition were studied. Furthermore, the correlation between thermal behaviors and electrochemical parameters in the process of TR triggered by overheat abuse was studied. From the microscopic point of view, the irreversible damage of TR to the active substances inside the battery, as well as the root cause of the intensification of heat production and the decline of electrochemical performance were revealed. The main conclusions were drawn as follows:

(1) With the progress of the cycle, the discharge capacity of each cell has different degrees of attenuation, and the attenuation degree of NCM811 cell is the most serious. The maximum temperature and temperature rise rate of NCM811 at room temperature at 2.0 C rate were 70.9 °C and 1.5556 °C /min, respectively. Compared with NCM523 and NCM622, the maximum temperature of NCM811 increased by 25% and 3%, and the temperature rise rate increased by 51.6% and 8.4%, respectively. Under higher temperature conditions, compared with NCM523 and NCM622, the maximum temperature and temperature rise rate of NCM811 are 96.6 °C and 1.4597 °C /min, respectively. The heat production behavior of cells with higher nickel content is the most severe, especially under high temperature and high discharge rate conditions.

(2) During the TR process, the T1, T2 and T3 of NCM523 were 81.3 °C, 184.3 °C, 574.94 °C. The T1, T2 and T3 of NCM622 were 80.8 °C, 171.7 °C, 670.63 °C respectively. For NCM811, the T1, T2 and T3 reached 78.4 °C, 139.9 °C and 676.44 °C, respectively. The temperature rose rates of NCM523, NCM622 and NCM811 came to 4264.71 °C /min, 9069.1 °C /min, and 9420.33 °C /min, respectively. With the increase of nickel content, the thermal safety of NCM811 is the most drastic.

(3) With the increase of nickel content, the damage degree of the anode material and its active substance of the cells before and after TR also increases, among which NCM811 has the most serious damage. The negative electrode morphology of NCM811 were deformed from a macroscopic point of view. From a microscopic point of view, the separation of the negative electrode materials from the Cu-collector was also the most severe. Hence, the damage of the internal morphology and crystal structure of the electrode materials is the fundamental cause of the degradation of electrochemical performance and the enhancement of heat production, which seriously hinders the normal electrochemical reaction and reduces the energy storage capacity of power batteries. The results of this study contribute to the optimization of electrode materials and the designation of thermal management systems.
